text:
A lot can happen in one week, as Australia's Matthew Ebden learned at the Olympics. The doubles world No. 3 got into the singles draw as an alternate following withdrawals and was thrashed by Novak Djokovic 6-0 6-1.

Immediately after the match, Ebdenannounced that he had officially retired from singles tennisand turned his attention to the men's doubles draw with John Peers.

Seven days later, the Aussies won an Olympic gold medal, coming from a set and break down to beat Austin Krajicek and Rajeev Ram of the USA in a match tiebreak 6-7(6) 7-6(1) [10-8].

Ebden looked lost on the court when he came out to face top seed Djokovic in singles last Saturday. It took 46 minutes for him to win his first and only game of the match after training 0-6 0-4. Early in the second set,he even resorted to trying to quit the match, jokingly handing his racket to a spectator so he could sub out.

The 36-year-old hadn't played a singles match in almost two years, since an outing in the qualifying rounds of the ATP 250 event in Winston-Salem. And he officially quit the game afterwards. "Safe to say I'm officially retired from singles after that," he wrote onInstagram.

After turning his attention back to his real specialism, doubles, he managed to win his first gold medal alongside compatriot and neighbour Peers. After securing the win, Ebden confessed that his loss to Djokovic was on his mind. " Honestly, yes. I think last night, I did think of it a bit," he said.

Just in:Olympic gold medallist 'really sad' after 'destroying friendship' with victory

The doubles star was even pre-planning a celebratory social media post. "I was actually dreaming of an Instagram post like how it started, how it’s going. I was thinking of my score [seven] days ago, winning one game, and then I was thinking swipe right and there’s a gold medal photo. Maybe I’ll have to make that post at some point," he laughed.

Ebden also revealed that he defied orders from Australia's tennis captain for the Olympics, Lleyton Hewitt, to get the chance to officially see off his singles career. He continued: "I did come off that singles match fully focused on the doubles. Literally when I got the call up to play [singles], Lleyton was like, ‘No don’t play, you save your body, make sure don’t get injured. You’re not here for that, I want a medal in the doubles or the mixed, whatever.’

"And I said, ‘Okay, I’ll play but I promise I won’t get injured.’ So my main goal that match was just not to get injured and I did that, had a hit, had some fun. Got to officially retire from singles which was nice. My wife was liking that, I never officially played my last official retirement singles match and she thought I deserved a nice little match like that. So that was a nice little miracle that happened to say goodbye to my singles career."

His focus on the doubles paid off, and Ebden and Peers walked away with their first gold medal. Peers had previously won bronze in the mixed doubles with Ash Barty in Tokyo. Ebden added: "It’s more than a dream, I didn’t even dream of winning a gold medal growing up or whatever. The last few years, had great success on the doubles court and it gave me a lot of belief and confidence coming in here.

"We spoke a few months ago about our partnership, our best chance to win and he said, what are you looking for? I said looking for our best chance to win. We believed in ourselves, we started a few months ago already thinking and preparing about this. Even all week here, all the tough matches so I’m super happy to get the reward because you don’t always get the reward for all of your hard work, but when you do it’s pretty sweet."

title: Olympics star who 'retired' with Novak Djokovic thrashing wins gold medal a week later | Tennis | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Yasmin Syed
description: Matthew Ebden went from agony to ecstasy as he won his first gold medal at the Olympics.
